elements.sent-2411-001: The Audio-Visual Event Localization (AVEL) task aims to temporally locate and classify video events that are both audible and visible.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most research in this field assumes a closed-set setting, which restricts these models' ability to handle test data containing event categories absent (unseen) during training.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recently, a few studies have explored AVEL in an open-set setting, enabling the recognition of unseen events as ``unknown'', but without providing category-specific semantics.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we advance the field by introducing the Open-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Event Localization (OV-AVEL) problem, which requires localizing audio-visual events and predicting explicit categories for both seen and unseen test data at inference.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address this new task, we propose the OV-AVEBench dataset, comprising 24,800 videos across 67 real-life audio-visual scenes (seen:unseen = 46:21), each with manual segment-level annotation.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also establish three evaluation metrics for this task.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we investigate two baseline approaches, one training-free and one using a further fine-tuning paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-008: Specifically, we utilize the unified multimodal space from the pretrained ImageBind model to extract audio, visual, and textual (event classes) features.
elements.sent-2411-009: The training-free baseline then determines predictions by comparing the consistency of audio-text and visual-text feature similarities.
elements.sent-2411-010: The fine-tuning baseline incorporates lightweight temporal layers to encode temporal relations within the audio and visual modalities, using OV-AVEBench training data for model fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-011: We evaluate these baselines on the proposed OV-AVEBench dataset and discuss potential directions for future work in this new field.
elements.sent-2411-012: The dataset and codes will be available at \url{https://github.com/jasongief/OV-AVEL .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{./figures/fig1_intro_v3.pdf} \caption{{ (a) Illustration of the AVEL task, which aims to temporally localize segments containing events that are both audible and visible, and identify their categories. (b) Studies of AVEL in different settings. In contrast to previous closed-set and open-set settings, we explore a more practical open-vocabulary AVEL problem, which needs to infer explicit event categories for both seen and unseen test data (\ie, data containing classes seen and unseen during training). Each color represents a distinct event class.} } \label{fig:introduction}
elements.sent-2411-013: Audio-visual learning, an essential sub-field of multimodal learning, has garnered increasing attention in recent years, resulting in the development of various research tasks, such as speech recognition~\cite{hu2016temporal,afouras2018deep,hong2023watch,dai2024study and separation~\cite{lee2021looking,rahimi2022reading,cheng2023filter,pan2024ravss , sound-source localization ~\cite{qian2020multiple, chen2021localizing, mo2022localizing, mahmud2024t and segmentation~\cite{zhou2022avs,mao2023multimodal,mo2024weakly,bhosale2024unsupervised,guo2023avis,zhou2024avss , audio-visual video parsing~\cite{tian2020unified, gao2023collecting, lai2023modality,zhou2023improving,zhou2024label,zhou2024vaplan and question answering~\cite{yun2021pano,li2022learning,yang2022avqa,shen2023fine,li2023progressive,li2024object .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we focus on a fundamental research task of Audio-Visual Event Localization (AVEL)~\cite{tian2018audio .}
elements.sent-2411-015: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:introduction (a), given a video containing both audio and visual streams, the AVEL task aims to temporally localize segments that contain an audio-visual event (\ie, both audible and visible) and identify its category.}
elements.sent-2411-016: For segments that do not satisfy this condition (\ie, only audible/visible or neither), their category is assigned to a special background class.
elements.sent-2411-017: In other words, this task requires perceiving the semantic alignment between audio and visual modalities, known as audio-visual correspondence~\cite{arandjelovic2017look,arandjelovic2018objects .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In recent years, there has been rapid advancement in the AVEL field: {1) Closed-Set Audio-Visual Event Localization.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Since the pioneering work~\cite{tian2018audio , numerous significant research works have been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, these methods aim to improve audio-visual fusion~\cite{tian2018audio,wu2019dual,xu2020cross,lin2019dual,duan2021audio,he2024cace,zhou2021positive , better distinguish the {background ~\cite{xia2022cross,zhou2022cpsp , and localize more precise temporal boundaries~\cite{yu2021mm,wu2022span,mahmud2023ave .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: While these methods achieve satisfactory performance in traditional AVEL tasks, they are designed for a closed-set scenario.
elements.sent-2411-022: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:introduction (b), methods in this setting can only infer data with event classes encountered or seen during model training (referred to as seen test data in our work), making it hard for unseen test data (namely test data with classes unseen during training) processing.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Recently, Yu~\etal began exploring the AVEL task in an open-set setting~\cite{yu2024openave .}
elements.sent-2411-024: To the best of our knowledge, this is currently the only work in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, the open-set AVEL needs to handle both seen and unseen test data at inference.
elements.sent-2411-026: For the {unseen test data with novel classes unseen in training, the model should recognize it as ``unknown'' rather than classifying it into a known category.}
elements.sent-2411-027: By employing evidential deep learning and positive-unlabeled learning, ~\cite{yu2024openave can identify unknown events in unseen test data.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, the model remains unable to determine specific categories for unseen events.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, its model evaluation is conducted on a limited subset of the relatively small AVE~\cite{tian2018audio dataset, where only 7 classes are treated as unknown, limiting its applicability in real-world scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, we investigate the {Open-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Event Localization (OV-AVEL) problem, a novel and more practical extension of AVEL.}
elements.sent-2411-031: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:introduction (b), OV-AVEL seeks to predict explicit event categories for both seen and unseen test data during inference, instead of assigning a general unknown class to unseen data as in open-set AVEL, thus providing more detailed temporal localization results.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Notably, event categories in unseen test data are not present during model training.
elements.sent-2411-033: A related topic to OV-AVEL is Audio-Visual Zero-Shot Learning (AV-ZSL)~\cite{parida2020coordinated,mazumder2021avgzslnet,mercea2022audio,mercea2022temporal,mo2024audio , which aims to classify unseen videos during testing by integrating both audio and visual signals.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The main difference is that AV-ZSL only needs to determine the category of the entire video, whereas our OV-AVEL seeks more fine-grained classification at the temporal level, requiring more precise recognition of audio-visual correspondence (\ie, perceiving the event category for each modality at each segment).
elements.sent-2411-035: To support this new task, we develop a corresponding dataset named {OV-AVEBench .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Compared to the AVE~\cite{tian2018audio dataset used in closed-set and open-set AVEL, our OV-AVEBench offers a broader range of video and event categories.}
elements.sent-2411-037: An overall comparison is presented in Table~\ref{tab:dataset_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, the proposed OV-AVEBench includes 24,800 videos in total, approximately 6 times the number in AVE~\cite{tian2018audio dataset; The videos in our OV-AVEBench encompass 67 classes of audio-visual events, whereas the AVE dataset includes only 28.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Moreover, each video sample in OV-AVEBench is manually annotated at the segment level, providing precise labels for model training or fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-040: These efforts in dataset construction allow us to explore more training data (seen classes) and unseen test data (unseen classes), facilitating model development and evaluation for real applications.
elements.sent-2411-041: Details about data collection, annotation, and splitting will be presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:ov-avebench .}
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition, we standardize the {evaluation metrics for the studied OV-AVEL task.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Prior AVEL studies typically adopt accuracy~\cite{tian2018audio as the evaluation metric, which segment-wisely compares predictions and ground truths.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This metric does not account for the recall and may not be intuitive in evaluating predicted events across different temporal scales.
elements.sent-2411-045: Inspired by the metrics in the audio-visual video parsing task~\cite{tian2020unified , we incorporate the F1-score as an additional evaluation metric for OV-AVEL, measuring it at both the segment-level and event-level.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The segment-level F1-score is calculated by segment-wise comparison of predictions with ground truths.
elements.sent-2411-047: Notably, the event-level metric treats consecutive segments with identical predictions as a complete event and computes the F1-score by assessing whether the Intersection over Union (IoU) between the predicted whole event and ground truth event exceeds the threshold of 0.5.
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus, this metric is stricter in evaluating the temporal boundaries of predictions.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Comparison of our OV-AVEBench with other datasets used in various AVEL settings.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|l|cc|ccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Settings} \multirow{2}{*}{Dataset} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Video} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Class} \cmidrule(r){3-7} total training total seen unseen closed-set~\cite{tian2018audio} AVE~\cite{tian2018audio} 4,143 3,309 28 28 0 open-set~\cite{yu2024openave} AVE~\cite{tian2018audio} 4,143 2,505 28 21 7 open-vocabulary OV-AVEBench 24,800 13,182 67 46 21 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:dataset_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-049: With the OV-AVEBench dataset and evaluation metrics established, we also propose preliminary baselines to address the OV-AVEL problem.
elements.sent-2411-050: To facilitate the recognition of various event classes, particularly those pertaining to unseen test data, we consider leveraging the zero-shot capability of recent language-based multimodal contrastive models.
elements.sent-2411-051: The language words are easily extendable and are not confined to predefined concepts (or categories for event classification).
elements.sent-2411-052: By applying contrastive learning to large-scale multimodal data pairs, the resulting embeddings can capture discriminative and accurate semantics.
elements.sent-2411-053: We opt to utilize ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind because it establishes a joint embedding space across multiple modalities, aligning well with the studied OV-AVEL task.}
elements.sent-2411-054: After extracting the segment-level audio, visual, and text embeddings using ImageBind (where the text represents all potential seen and unseen event classes), we initially explore a simple training-free baseline.
elements.sent-2411-055: Specifically, we compute the cosine similarity matrices for audio-text and visual-text features, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this way, we can identify the predicted event category for each audio and visual segment, corresponding to the highest similarity value, and subsequently generate audio-visual event predictions by verifying the consistency of the predicted audio and visual event categories.
elements.sent-2411-057: Notably, this baseline is training-free, directly operating on the test data.
elements.sent-2411-058: To utilize the annotated training data from the proposed OV-AVEBench dataset, we further explore a fine-tuning baseline.
elements.sent-2411-059: Although the unseen test data and training data possess distinct event categories, the temporal information in training data, which reflects the continuity of various audio-visual events, remains beneficial for the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-060: Inspired by this, we incorporate some lightweight transformer layers into the ImageBind model to learn temporal relations within audio and visual modalities.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then, we fine-tune the model using the training data.
elements.sent-2411-062: Notably, parameters of the vanilla ImageBind model remain frozen, with only those of the temporal layers being learnable; thus, the increase in resource or computational load is relatively limited.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our experiments demonstrate that the fine-tuning baseline significantly outperforms the training-free version in inference on both seen and unseen test data.
elements.sent-2411-064: In summary, our main contributions are three-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-065: We propose the Open-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Event Localization (OV-AVEL) task, aiming to localize both seen and unseen audio-visual events in test videos.
elements.sent-2411-066: To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to advance the AVEL area toward more practical applications in open-vocabulary scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-067: To facilitate this new task, we construct the OV-AVEBench dataset, which includes segment-level manual event annotations.
elements.sent-2411-068: Besides, we establish standard evaluation metrics that encompass typical {accuracy , as well as segment-level and event-level F1-scores.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We present two simple baselines: one adopting a training-free paradigm, which can be upgraded through further fine-tuning on available training data.
elements.sent-2411-070: We hope that our benchmark will inspire future research in this field.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./figures/fig2_dataset_statistic_v5.pdf} \caption{Statistics about the proposed OV-AVEBench dataset. (a) Our OV-AVEBench contains 24,800 videos covering 67 practical audio-visual scenes from the real world. Each event category and its corresponding video amount are listed. The category highlighted by a black bounding box indicates that data in that category is only available during the inference phase (unseen classes/data). (b) The audio-visual events in the videos exhibit various temporal scales, with some containing only background. We also visualize the category distribution (c), the video distribution (d) of the seen and unseen data, and the video counts for the training, validation, and test sets (e). } \label{fig:dataset_statistic}
elements.sent-2411-071: We construct the OV-AVEBench dataset to facilitate the study of Open-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Event Localization (OV-AVEL).
elements.sent-2411-072: In Table~\ref{tab:dataset_comparison , we have provided a basic overview of OV-AVEBench.}
elements.sent-2411-073: In the following subsections, we share more details about data collection, annotation, and splitting.
elements.sent-2411-074: The target audio-visual events in the OV-AVEL task necessitate semantic correlation between the audio and visual modalities (in at least some video segments).
elements.sent-2411-075: To meet this requirement and avoid unnecessary costs, we resort to existing VGGSound~\cite{chen2020vggsound dataset, a large-scale audio-visual dataset in our community that provides ample video resources.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Specifically, VGGSound dataset consists of over 200k videos covering 309 audio classes.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, some classes may be easily recorded in audio signal but are difficult to represent in visual frames, such as wind noise and thunder.
elements.sent-2411-078: Additionally, some classes are either too similar or too fine-grained (\eg, car engine starting vs. car engine idling), or too rare in current real-life (\eg, dinosaurs bellowing).
elements.sent-2411-079: We filtered out categories like these and ultimately selected {67 common and suitable classes for constructing the OV-AVEBench dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The complete category list of selected categories is presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (a).}
elements.sent-2411-081: These event categories correspond to five major groups: human activity, animal activity, musical instruments, vehicles, and several other audio-visual scenes from real life.
elements.sent-2411-082: After determining the event categories, we downloaded the corresponding videos based on the YouTube URLs provided by the VGGSound dataset.
elements.sent-2411-083: A small number of videos were not currently available.
elements.sent-2411-084: Next, five volunteers were invited to manually review and check the downloadeded videos.
elements.sent-2411-085: Some low-quality videos (\eg, those that were completely mismatched with their category tags) were further removed.
elements.sent-2411-086: After these steps, we ultimately retained {24,800 videos as the data resources for our OV-AVEBench dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The specific number of videos in each event category is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (a).}
elements.sent-2411-089: We examine the temporal length of audio-visual events contained in the videos.
elements.sent-2411-090: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (b), we find that 43.48\% video data contain audio-visual events in all temporal segments (10 seconds), while 24.27\% contain no audio-visual events (0 seconds), \ie, containing background class, and over one-third of the data fall in between.}
elements.sent-2411-091: These video data require the model to recognize various event categories, distinguish background segments, and localize events across different temporal scales, making our OV-AVEBench dataset applicable to the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-092: After obtaining the videos, we attempt to provide audio-visual event labels for them.
elements.sent-2411-093: For each video, we divide it into ten 1-second segments.
elements.sent-2411-094: The intermediate video frame of each segment is extracted to represent its visual component.
elements.sent-2411-095: The audio component is the corresponding 1-second audio sequence.
elements.sent-2411-096: Then, the audio-visual event labels can be determined by evaluating whether the visual frame and the audio sequence describe the same event.
elements.sent-2411-097: If they match, this segment is labeled as `1' with a meaningful event category, \eg, baby laughter; otherwise, it is labeled as `0' and categorized as background.
elements.sent-2411-098: This process allows us to obtain segment-level labels.
elements.sent-2411-099: To ensure high-quality labels for the community, we conducted the annotation process through crowdsourcing.
elements.sent-2411-100: Ten human annotators were involved in this process.
elements.sent-2411-101: First, we provided video examples along with guidelines to ensure that the annotators understood the annotation procedures and standards.
elements.sent-2411-103: After finishing the annotation, we exchanged annotators to perform a second-round re-evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-104: Annotations with differing opinions were discussed to reach a final judgment.
elements.sent-2411-105: It took us about three weeks to complete the annotation process.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{./figures/fig3_framework_v2.pdf} \caption{Overview of the proposed baseline methods. We utilize the audio and image encoders of the pretrained Imagebind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind} (with frozen parameters) to extract segment-level audio and visual features. \ding{172} The training-free baseline sends texts of all candidate classes (both seen and unseen) to extract features. Then, the audio-visual event prediction is decided by evaluating the consistency between audio-text and visual-text feature similarities. \ding{173} The fine-tuning baseline additionally inserts some temporal layers into the audio and visual encoders to strengthen temporal interaction learning. This model is fine-tuned/trained with training data (with seen classes). Only the texts of seen classes are known and used in model fine-tuning, while the unseen classes are additionally introduced during inference. The final audio-visual event prediction is obtained by fusing event probabilities of audio and visual modalities. } \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-106: The OV-AVEL task requires handling both seen and unseen data, which means that only some event categories are present during training.
elements.sent-2411-107: Our OV-AVEBench dataset contains a total of 67 event classes.
elements.sent-2411-108: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (c), we select 46 classes as seen classes (appearing in training data) while the remaining 21 classes as unseen (only appearing in the evaluation phase).}
elements.sent-2411-109: The detailed category names can also be observed from Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (a), where the unseen classes are highlighted by black bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Importantly, we did not simply select 21 classes from the entire category list.
elements.sent-2411-111: Instead, we identify specific classes from each major audiovisual category group while carefully balancing the number of videos in the resulting seen and unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-112: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (d), 16,497 videos are finally used as seen data (whose classes are seen during training), and 8,303 videos are used as unseen data.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Notably, some of the seen data can also appear in the validation and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-114: The detailed numbers are presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset_statistic (e).}
elements.sent-2411-115: Specifically, we split the videos into training, validation, and test sets, with respective video counts of 13,182, 5,798, and 5,820.
elements.sent-2411-116: For the validation and test sets, we set the ratio of seen and unseen videos (\ie, videos with seen and unseen classes) at approximately 3:7.
elements.sent-2411-117: This allows us to evaluate models using more unseen data during the inference phase.
elements.sent-2411-118: We will release the OV-AVEBench dataset to the community for transparency.
elements.sent-2411-119: Given an audible video, it is divided into $T=10$ consecutive and non-overlapping segments, with $\{A_t\}_{t=1 ^T$ and $\{V_t\}_{t=1 ^T$ representing the audio and visual components, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The OV-AVEL task aims to localize video segments that contain an audio-visual event and identify their categories.
elements.sent-2411-121: Each video is typically dominated by one event category.
elements.sent-2411-122: The ground truth labels can be denoted as $\bm{Y =\{Y_t\}_{t=1 ^T \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C+1)}$, where `$C+1$' indicates the total number of audio-visual event classes plus a background class.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Notably, during the inference phase, the OV-AVEL task addresses data with both seen and unseen classes.
elements.sent-2411-124: We denote the total number of seen and unseen event classes as $C_s$ and $C_u$, respectively, where $C_s = 46$ and $C_u = 21$ in the proposed OV-AVEBench dataset.
elements.sent-2411-126: The OV-AVEL task can be addressed with a straightforward training-free baseline, as illustrated in the upper part of Fig.~\ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-127: First, we utilize the pretrained ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind model discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:intro to extract audio and visual features.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Specifically, the sampled video frame from each visual segment is sent to the image encoder of ImageBind, yielding the segment-level visual features $\bm{F _v = \{ \bm{v _t \}_{t=1 ^T \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$, where $d=1024$ is the feature dimension.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Similarly, each audio segment is sent to the audio encoder to extract audio features, denoted as $\bm{F _a = \{ \bm{a _t \}_{t=1 ^T \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Traditional approaches to closed-set AVEL~\cite{tian2018audio typically use only the audio and visual features as model inputs for event prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-131: To achieve open-vocabulary AVEL, we adopt a zero-shot classification paradigm similar to CLIP~\cite{radford2021CLIP .}
elements.sent-2411-132: We send all candidate event classes (seen and unseen) to the text encoder of ImageBind to obtain the text (event category) features $\bm{F _e = \{ \bm{e _c \}_{c=1 ^{C+1 \in \mathbb{R ^{(C+1) \times d}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Notably, we add a special text other that corresponds to the background class, handling situations that do not belong to the listed seen and unseen classes (other new potential event classes can also be flexibly added in practical applications).
elements.sent-2411-134: Next, we compute the cosine similarities of audio-text and visual-text features, denoted as $\bm{S _{ae \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C+1)}$ and $\bm{S _{ve \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C+1)}$, as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{S}_{ae} = \| \bm{F}_a \| \otimes \| \bm{F}_e \|^{\top}, ~~ \bm{S}_{ve} = \| \bm{F}_v \| \otimes \| \bm{F}_e \|^{\top}, \label{eq:cosine_simm}\end{equation} where $\| \cdot \|$ denotes L2-normalization and $\otimes$ is the matrix multiplication.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: By scanning each row of $\bm{S _{ae}$ and $\bm{S _{ve}$, we can predict the category of each audio and visual segment by identifying the category with the highest cosine similarity value (marked by pink boxes shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework}\ding{172 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: The audio-visual events in target segments require that the category of the audio segment and the synchronized visual segment should be identical.
elements.sent-2411-137: Therefore, we can easily determine the final audio-visual event predictions by checking the audio and visual class consistency for each segment: if both modalities share the same event category, that segment contains an audio-visual event of that category; otherwise, it is recognized as background.
elements.sent-2411-138: The baseline method described above is training-free since the parameters of the audio, image, and text encoders are frozen.
elements.sent-2411-139: Since segment-level labels are available for the training data (with seen classes), we attempt to enhance the training-free baseline through additional fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-140: ImageBind can provide advanced audiovisual features; however, they are independent at the segment level.
elements.sent-2411-141: The temporal relations across segments are also crucial for our OV-AVEL task because the target audio-visual events typically span various temporal scales.
elements.sent-2411-142: Motivated by this, we insert some learnable temporal layers after the audio encoder and image encoder of ImageBind to enhance the temporal interaction of each modality (illustrated in the lower part of Fig.~\ref{fig:framework ).}
elements.sent-2411-143: In practice, the temporal layers are implemented as a stack of $L$ standard Transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-144: We denote the generated audio and visual features as $\bm{F _a^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$ and $\bm{F _v^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$, respectively.. Training/Fine-tuning.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Notably, data in the training set contains only the seen classes.
elements.sent-2411-146: Therefore, during model training/fine-tuning, only the texts of $C_s$ seen classes and additional text other (outlined by the orange box in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework}\ding{173 ) are sent to the text encoder of ImageBind to extract text features, yielding $\bm{F _{e ^{s \in \mathbb{R ^{ (C_s+1) \times d}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Then, we can compute the feature similarity matrices of audio-text and visual-text pairs, similar to Eq.~\ref{eq:cosine_simm , denoted as $\bm{S _{ae ^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C_s+1)}$ and $\bm{S _{ve ^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C_s+1)}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: The matrices $\bm{S _{ae ^{'}$ and $\bm{S _{ve ^{'}$ reflect the category probabilities of audio events and visual events, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: We generate the final audio-visual event probability $\bm{S _{ave ^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C_s+1)}$ by fusing them as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{S}_{ave}^{'} = \sqrt{ \bm{S}_{ae}^{'} \odot \bm{S}_{ve}^{'} }, \label{eq:fine_tune}\end{equation} where $\odot$ is the Hadamard product.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: This strategy differs from the direct comparison of $\bm{S _{ae}$ and $\bm{S _{ve}$ used in the training-free baseline, which is non-differentiable for model back-propagation.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: The ground truth $\bm{Y ^{' \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C_s+1)}$ for the training data can be easily obtained by selecting columns of corresponding seen classes from $\bm{Y \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times (C+1)}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Then, our fine-tuning baseline is trained by optimizing the cross entropy loss between $\bm{S _{ave ^{'}$ and $\bm{Y ^{'}$.. Inference.}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: The OV-AVEL task involves handling both seen and unseen data (\ie, data with seen and unseen classes) during the inference phase.
elements.sent-2411-154: As highlighted by the yellow dotted box in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework}\ding{173 , the texts of both seen and unseen classes are sent to the text encoder for feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The processing of audio and visual modalities follows the same flow as in training, whereas the audio and visual segments are processed by the pretrained encoders and fine-tuned temporal layers to extract audio and visual features.
elements.sent-2411-156: Then, we can generate the probability of audio-visual events by utilizing audio-text and visual-text feature similarities as described in Eq.~\ref{eq:fine_tune .}
elements.sent-2411-157: The final prediction can be made by selecting the event category with the largest probability.
elements.sent-2411-158: We conduct experiments on the proposed OV-AVEBench dataset and evaluate the performance of our baselines using the three evaluation metrics introduced in Sec.~\ref{sec:intro , \ie, the accuracy (Acc.), segment-level F1-score (Seg.), and event-level F1-score (Eve.).}
elements.sent-2411-159: The average result of three metrics (Avg.) is also reported.
elements.sent-2411-160: In both baselines, we employ the parameters of the pretrained ImageBind\_Huge\footnote{\url{https://github.com/facebookresearch/ImageBind/tree/main , the only officially released version of the ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind model, to extract audio-visual-text features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: For the fine-tuning baseline, we set the batch size to 32 and fine-tune the model (learnable temporal layers) for 5 epochs; the Adam optimizer is used with a learning rate of 5e-5.
elements.sent-2411-162: All experiments are conducted on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090D (24GB) GPU.
elements.sent-2411-163: The source code will be released.
elements.sent-2411-164: We propose both a training-free baseline and a fine-tuning baseline to address the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-165: In Table~\ref{tab:main_results , we report the performances of both baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-166: The training-free baseline achieves 59.2\% in Acc., 46.7\% in Seg., and 34.0\% in Eve., resulting in an average performance of 46.6\%.
elements.sent-2411-167: The fine-tuning baseline significantly outperforms the training-free version, showing an 11.2\% improvement in the average metric.
elements.sent-2411-168: Moreover, we observe that: the training-free baseline model performs slightly better on the unseen test data; after fine-tuning on training data, the baseline model is improved in recognizing both seen and unseen test data (17.4\%$\uparrow$ and 8.8\%$\uparrow$ in Avg., respectively).
elements.sent-2411-169: The improvement is more pronounced for seen test data because their event classes have been seen in training.
elements.sent-2411-170: However, fine-tuning remains beneficial for unseen test data.
elements.sent-2411-171: We speculate that further fine-tuning helps the model learn temporal relations from training data, facilitating the adaption and updating of prior knowledge from ImageBind to downstream OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-172: This enables more precise localization of temporal boundaries for unseen test data.
elements.sent-2411-173: This is supported by the event-level metric, which significantly improves from 34.0\% to 47.5\%.
elements.sent-2411-174: We provide additional evidence and discussions on this in Sec.~\ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-175: In short, the comparison between the two baselines highlights the benefits of further fine-tuning, especially when some training data with annotations are available.

Seg. Eve. Avg. Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. total 59.2 46.7 34.0 46.6 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 seen 57.5 45.0 34.0 45.5 72.5 61.8 54.5 62.9 unseen 59.8 47.3 34.0 47.0 64.9 55.0 47.5 55.8 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:main_results}
elements.figure-2411-006: \tabcolsep 16pt \caption{Ablation study on the number of temporal layers $L$. Results are reported on the total test data.} \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c} $L$ Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. 1 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 2 65.4 {56.0} 49.2 56.9 3 62.8 54.0 {47.3} 54.7 \end{tabular} \label{tab:ablation_on_temporal_layers}
elements.sent-2411-177: In this section, we provide some ablation studies on the key configurations adopted in our fine-tuning baseline.
elements.sent-2411-179: We employ $L$ learnable temporal layers to enhance temporal interactions within audio and visual modalities.
elements.sent-2411-180: The results, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_on_temporal_layers , illustrate the impacts of varying the number of layers.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The model achieves the highest average performance using only one temporal layer.
elements.sent-2411-182: Increasing the number of temporal layers may make the model more complex and lead to overfitting, thus degrading the performance.
elements.sent-2411-183: Consequently, we identify $L=1$ to implement the temporal layer, which is lightweight and only introduces 8.4M trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-185: We utilize a special text other to assist the model in classifying events that do not belong to either the seen or unseen classes.
elements.sent-2411-186: We conduct an ablation study to explore its impact.
elements.sent-2411-187: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_on_other_class , the model using additional other class outperforms that baseline trained without other by 10.8\% in average performance.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The improvement is consistent across both seen and unseen test data.
elements.sent-2411-189: This underscores the superiority of introducing the additional class text other, which helps prevent the model from misclassifying unknown events or backgrounds as existing seen or unseen classes.
elements.sent-2411-190: In Sec.~\ref{sec:supp_A of our Appendix, we further show that the employment of other is slightly better than other choices like background.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \tabcolsep 3pt \caption{Ablation study on the employment of the text other.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c|ccc|c} \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell[c]{Data type}} \multicolumn{4}{c|}{w. other } \multicolumn{4}{c}{w/o other } \cmidrule(r){2-9} Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. total 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 59.3 46.9 34.9 47.0 seen 72.5 61.8 54.5 62.9 62.0 49.2 37.8 49.7 unseen 64.9 55.0 47.5 55.8 58.2 46.0 33.7 45.9 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:ablation_on_other_class}
elements.figure-2411-008: \tabcolsep 12pt \caption{\textbf{Ablation study on the strategies for predicting $\bm{S}_{ave}^{'}$}. Detailed implementation of each strategy is shown in the main text. Results are reported on the total test data.} \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c} Strategy Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. Prob-avg 45.1 38.7 33.3 39.0 Fea-avg 46.8 39.8 34.0 40.2 Sqrt (Eq.~\ref{eq:fine_tune}) 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 \end{tabular} \label{tab:ablation_on_ave_generation}
elements.sent-2411-192: In our fine-tuning baseline, we generate the audio-visual event probability $\bm{S _{ave ^{'}$ by computing the square root of predicted audio event probability $\bm{S _{ae ^{'}$ and visual event probability $\bm{S _{ve ^{'}$ (Eq.~\ref{eq:fine_tune ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: We refer to this strategy as Sqrt.
elements.sent-2411-194: Furthermore, we explore two additional variants to obtain $\bm{S _{ave ^{'}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: We re-train the fine-tuning baseline model using these strategies and evaluate the model on the test set.
elements.sent-2411-196: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_on_ave_generation , the Sqrt strategy significantly outperforms the Prob-avg and Fea-avg variants.}
elements.sent-2411-197: The geometric mean used by Sqrt is more effective than arithmetic mean at preventing audiovisual event predictions from being misled by a high-probability prediction in one modality.
elements.sent-2411-198: These results indicate the importance of design in generating final audio-visual event probabilities, a factor that future research should also consider.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this section, we provide further results and analyses about our proposed baselines and the studied OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-200: We hope the findings and discussions inspire future work.
elements.figure-2411-009: \tabcolsep 3pt \caption{Comparison of using temporal layers and linear layers in fine-tuning baseline.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c|ccc|c} \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell[c]{Data type}} \multicolumn{4}{c|}{Temporal layer} \multicolumn{4}{c}{ Linear layer} \cmidrule(r){2-9} Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. total 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 46.6 38.0 32.4 39.0 seen 72.5 61.8 54.5 {62.9} 76.2 64.5 56.9 65.8 unseen 64.9 55.0 47.5 55.8 34.8 27.4 22.6 28.3 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:temporal_linear_layers}
elements.figure-2411-010: \tabcolsep 12pt \caption{Temporal interactions in intra- and cross- modalities for model fine-tuning. Results are reported on the total test data.} \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c} Cases Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. intra only 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 cross only 54.4 45.9 39.2 46.5 intra + cross 63.5 54.3 47.1 55.0 \end{tabular} \label{tab:ablation_on_intra_cross_attention}
elements.sent-2411-202: Our fine-tuning baseline employs some temporal layers utilizing the self-attention mechanism in Transformer to enhance temporal interactions across video segments.
elements.sent-2411-203: Here, we replace these with learnable linear layers to update audio/visual features segment-wisely (\ie, without temporal interactions).
elements.sent-2411-204: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:temporal_linear_layers , the average performance of the model fine-tuned using linear layers lags considerably behind that using temporal layers.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Specifically, we find that the linear layers are slightly more effective than temporal layers for event localization of seen test data but are significantly inferior for unseen test data (27.5\% $\downarrow$ in Avg.. metric).
elements.sent-2411-206: These results suggest that 1) for seen test data with classes present during training, simple linear layers may be adequate for fine-tuning; while 2) for unseen test data, sophisticated temporal relation modeling on training data becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-207: Consequently, developing more versatile and robust network architectures would be an intriguing area for future research.
elements.sent-2411-209: The temporal layers in our fine-tuning baseline facilitate temporal interactions within the audio and visual modalities (intra-modal).
elements.sent-2411-210: We also attempted to insert some temporal layers to capture cross-modal temporal relations.
elements.sent-2411-211: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_on_intra_cross_attention , adding cross-modal temporal layers does not yield improvements.}
elements.sent-2411-212: We speculate that the audio and visual features extracted by the pretrained ImageBind model can provide explicit and precise semantics of audio events and visual events, reducing the need for cross-modal interactions.
elements.sent-2411-213: By focusing on the temporal interactions in intra-modality, the model can achieve satisfactory performance.

Seg. Eve. Avg. \multirow{3}{*}{100\%} total 67.1 56.9 49.5 57.8 \multirow{3}{*}{1} seen 72.5 61.8 54.5 62.9 unseen 64.9 55.0 47.5 55.8 \multirow{3}{*}{75\%} total 66.1 56.9 49.9 57.6 \multirow{3}{*}{3} seen 75.1 65.4 59.3 66.6 unseen 62.5 53.5 46.1 54.0 \multirow{3}{*}{50\%} total 66.7 57.1 49.7 57.8 \multirow{3}{*}{5} seen 75.3 66.0 59.9 67.1 unseen 63.5 53.5 45.6 54.2 \multirow{3}{*}{25\%} total 66.4 56.8 49.8 57.7 \multirow{3}{*}{6} seen 73.0 62.6 55.7 63.8 unseen 63.8 54.4 47.5 55.2 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:diff_ratios_of_training_data}

Seg. Eve. Avg. Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. total 48.9 39.1 29.8 39.3 59.2 46.7 34.0 46.6 seen 50.1 40.6 32.0 40.9 57.5 45.0 34.0 45.5 unseen 48.5 38.5 29.0 38.6 59.8 47.3 34.0 47.0 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:comparsion_with_LLM}
elements.sent-2411-214: Different ratios of training data used for model fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-215: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:diff_ratios_of_training_data , we fine-tune the baseline model with various ratios of training data (sampling data for each training class accordingly).}
elements.sent-2411-216: Interestingly, we find that the model achieves similar average performance across different data ratios.
elements.sent-2411-217: For instance, using only 25\% of the training data, the model performance can reach 57.7\% in Avg., close to that achieved with 100\% training data.
elements.sent-2411-218: The cost of using less training data is that more training/fine-tuning epochs are required.
elements.sent-2411-219: Additionally, we find that while using 100\% of training data is more beneficial for unseen test data recognition, using 50\% of training data is the most effective for seen test data recognition.
elements.sent-2411-220: We conjecture that more training data may cause the problem of overfitting for seen classes.
elements.sent-2411-221: Therefore, determining a more balanced training strategy to optimize both seen and unseen data recognition would be a valuable direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-223: Currently, many LLMs are designed for video understanding, though few can handle and analyze both audio and visual inputs simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-224: We evaluate a recently proposed, open-source audio-visual LLM, Video-LLaMA2~\cite{cheng2024videollama , on our OV-AVEBench dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Table~\ref{tab:comparsion_with_LLM suggests that the zero-shot performance of Video-LLaMA2 is inferior to our training-free baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-226: While Video-LLaMA2 can describe events within {entire audio sequence, it faces challenges in achieving segment-level perception of audio-visual alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-227: By emphasizing this limitation, we believe that potential LLM-based solutions can be developed for the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-228: We propose the Open-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Event Localization (OV-AVEL) task, advancing the traditional closed-set AVEL problem into more practical open-vocabulary scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-229: Accordingly, we meticulously construct the OV-AVEBench dataset, making efforts in data collection, annotation, and splitting.
elements.sent-2411-230: We hope that the OV-AVEBench will serve as a standardized testbed for future research on OV-AVEL.
elements.sent-2411-231: Furthermore, we introduce two baseline approaches, a training-free baseline and a fine-tuning baseline, to address this new task.
elements.sent-2411-232: We present some discussions based on our experimental findings, which we expect will inspire future advancements in the OV-AVEL field.

elements.sent-2411-233: In Sec.~\ref{sec:ablations_main_paper of our main paper, we have demonstrated that our baseline models using additional class text other outperform models that do not use other.}
elements.sent-2411-234: Here, we further compare the employment of other with another option, namely background.
elements.sent-2411-235: The experimental results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_on_other_class .}
elements.sent-2411-236: For both the training-free and fine-tuning baselines, the use of other is slightly better than background.
elements.sent-2411-237: Compared to background, we speculate that the text other can further help the model deal with situations that include other meaningful event classes not listed in the seen and unseen class texts.
elements.section-2411-007: Comparison between the Training-free Baseline with Another Variant
elements.sent-2411-238: The training-free baseline introduced in our main paper utilizes ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind to extract audio, visual, and textual embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-239: It computes the audio-text and visual-text feature (cosine) similarities to determine final audio-visual event predictions.
elements.sent-2411-240: We refer to this strategy as joint since multimodal features are extracted from a shared feature space.
elements.sent-2411-241: Furthermore, we compare this approach with another variant, where the audio-text and visual-text feature similarities are calculated using feature embeddings from separate backbones.
elements.sent-2411-242: Specifically, for each segment, the pretrained CLAP~\cite{wu2023clap model is used to extract the audio and text features to generate the audio-text feature similarity; the pretrained CLIP~\cite{radford2021CLIP model is used to extract the visual and text features to generate the visual-text feature similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Notably, the text encoders of CLAP and CLIP models are different, so the text features are extracted independently.
elements.sent-2411-244: After obtaining the audio-text and visual-text feature similarities, we identify the event categories of the audio segments and visual segments based on the highest similarity values.
elements.sent-2411-245: The final audio-visual event prediction can be made by comparing the consistency of the predicted audio and visual event categories.
elements.sent-2411-246: The experimental results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:joint_clipclap .}
elements.sent-2411-247: The joint baseline model using ImageBind significantly outperforms the separate variant, with improvements of 4.9\%, 3.9\%, and 5.2\% in the Avg.
elements.sent-2411-248: These results indicate the advantages of adopting a joint feature space for multimodal feature embedding, which can better capture semantic alignment among multiple modalities for the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-249: Dataset The AVE dataset is constructed for the closed-set AVEL task~\cite{tian2018audio .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Here, we directly apply our two baseline models to the test set of AVE dataset in a zero-shot inference manner.
elements.sent-2411-251: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:exp_on_ave_dataset , the fine-tuning baseline continues to outperform the training-free version, demonstrating results competitive with the prior unsupervised state-of-the-art (SOTA) method CMLCL~\cite{bao2023cross .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Notably, CMLCL still uses unlabeled videos of the training set in the AVE dataset.
elements.sent-2411-253: Moreover, if further fine-tuning our baseline model on the AVE dataset, the model can reach 79.6\% accuracy without sophisticated designs, approaching the performance of fully-supervised AVEL methods~\cite{tian2018audio,yu2021mm,zhou2022cpsp,xia2022cross,ge2023learning .}
elements.sent-2411-254: Nevertheless, we encourage readers to focus on the intrinsic differences: our method is designed for the open-vocabulary AVEL, while prior SOTA methods are tailored specifically for closed-set AVEL.
elements.figure-2411-014: \tabcolsep 3pt \caption{Comparison between the Training-free baseline with another variant. The default implementation in our main paper uses ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind} to jointly extract multimodal features and generate audio-visual event predictions. In contrast, the separate variant uses the pretrained CLAP~\cite{wu2023clap} and CLIP~\cite{radford2021CLIP} models to extract features independently and computes the audio-text and visual-text feature similarities separately.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccc|c|ccc|c} \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell[c]{Data type}} \multicolumn{4}{c|}{ImageBind (joint)} \multicolumn{4}{c}{CLAP\&CLIP (separate)} \cmidrule(r){2-9} Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. Acc. Seg. Eve. Avg. total 59.2 46.7 34.0 46.6 51.5 41.9 31.7 41.7 seen 57.5 45.0 34.0 45.5 51.4 41.4 31.9 41.6 unseen 59.8 47.3 34.0 47.0 51.6 42.2 31.6 41.8 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:joint_clipclap}
elements.figure-2411-015: \tabcolsep 12pt \caption{\textbf{Zero-shot evaluation on AVE~\cite{tian2018audio} dataset.}} \begin{tabular}{l|c|c} Manners Methods Acc. \multirow{2}{*}{zero-shot} training-free (our) 54.8 fine-tuning (our) 61.9 {\color{gray}{unsupervised}} {\color{gray}CMLCL~\cite{bao2023cross}} {\color{gray}63.2} \end{tabular} \label{tab:exp_on_ave_dataset}
elements.section-2411-009: Class-wise Performance of the Proposed Two Baselines
elements.sent-2411-255: In Table~\ref{tab:main_results of our main paper, we present the overall performance of the proposed training-free and fine-tuning baselines on the test set.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Here, we further report their performance on each individual event class.
elements.sent-2411-257: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:class_wise_performance , the fine-tuning baseline outperforms the training-free baseline in most event classes (approximately 56 out of 67) across all evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-258: This highlights the benefits of additional fine-tuning on training data.
elements.sent-2411-259: Moreover, we observe that some event classes, such as slot machine and chicken crowing, remain challenging for prediction, suggesting avenues for further improvement in future work.
elements.section-2411-010: More Details on Prompts for adapting Video-LLaMA2 to our OV-AVEL task
elements.sent-2411-260: In Table~\ref{tab:comparsion_with_LLM of our main paper, we compare the training-free baseline with an advanced audio-visual LLM, namely Video-LLaMA2~\cite{cheng2024videollama .}}
elements.sent-2411-261: Video-LLaMA2 can process video frames and, more importantly, it can handle general audio signals that are not limited to human speech, unlike other audio-visual LLMs~\cite{munasinghe2023pg .}
elements.sent-2411-262: This makes it particularly suitable for the studied OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-263: Here, we provide more details on the prompts for adapting Video-LLaMA2 for the OV-AVEL task.
elements.sent-2411-264: Specifically, we tried several prompts and found the following prompt to be the most robust and effective for making predictions: ``Instruction: For the given 10-second video, divide it into 10 one-second segments.
elements.sent-2411-265: For each segment, if its audio and visual streams describe the same event, assign the label ``x" as ``1"; otherwise, label this segment as ``0".
elements.sent-2411-266: User request: After processing all 10 video segments, you will obtain a list with 10 elements, each element being either ``1" or ``0" according to the above Instruction.
elements.sent-2411-267: Finally, return the most relevant event category of the video from the candidate category list: [``airplane flyby", ``ambulance siren", ``arc welding", ``baby laughter", ``basketball bounce", ``bird chirping", ``bowling impact", ``cat purring", ``cattle mooing", ``chainsawing trees", ``chicken crowing", ...(notebly, all event category texts should be listed; here, we omit the remaining ones for simplicity )].
elements.sent-2411-268: The output format should be: ``ave:" A python list [x, x, x, x, x, x, x, x, x, x] (replace ``x" with ``1" or ``0" according to the prediction); Insert a line break.
elements.sent-2411-269: In this way, we can obtain the audio-visual event predictions of each test video and compare its performance with the proposed training-free baseline, as reported in Table 9 of our main paper.
elements.sent-2411-270: Additionally, we display some qualitative results in Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_1 and Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_2 and provide more discussions in Sec.~\ref{sec:qualitative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: We finally present some intuitive video examples for OV-AVEL, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_1 and Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: Specifically, we visualize the predictions generated by Video-LLaMA2~\cite{cheng2024videollama , along with the proposed training-free and fine-tuning baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-273: As shown in the figures, the proposed fine-tuning baseline generally yields more accurate temporal localization results for both seen and unseen events/videos.
elements.sent-2411-274: For instance, in the three examples shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_1 , Video-LLaMA2 tends to predict most video segments as background, indicating its limitation in accurately perceiving the audio-visual correspondence at a fine-grained temporal-level.}
elements.sent-2411-275: Although the training-free baseline performs better than Video-LLaMA2, the predictions for some video segments remain unsatisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-276: In contrast, the fine-tuning baseline performs better in localizing temporal segments containing audio-visual events and classifying the event categories.
elements.sent-2411-277: Similar phenomena can also be observed from Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-278: These qualitative results, along with the quantitative results presented in our main paper, suggest the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed fine-tuning baseline.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{./figures/supp_class_wise_performance.pdf} \caption{Detailed performance of the proposed two baselines on each event class. } \label{fig:class_wise_performance}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./figures/supp_avel_examples_1abc.pdf} \caption{Qualitative examples for seen audio-visual event localization. } \label{fig:qualitative_1}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./figures/supp_avel_examples_2abc.pdf} \caption{Qualitative examples for unseen audio-visual event localization. } \label{fig:qualitative_2}
elements.sent-2411-279: Audio-visual learning, an essential sub-field of multimodal learning, has garnered increasing attention in recent years, resulting in the development of various research tasks, such as sound-source localization ~\cite{qian2020multiple, chen2021localizing,guo2024unitr,guo2024instance,mo2022localizing,mahmud2024t and segmentation~\cite{zhou2022avs,zhou2023avss,hao2024improving,mao2023contrastive,mao2023multimodal,guo2023avis,guo2024open , audio-visual video parsing~\cite{tian2020unified,yu2021mm,gao2023collecting,zhou2023improving,zhou2024label,zhou2024vaplan,zhao2024multimodal,chen2024cm and generation~\cite{shen2023fine,mao2024tavgbench,li2024tri , question answering~\cite{li2022learning,yang2022avqa,li2023progressive,li2024object,li2024patch , \etc. In this paper, we focus on a fundamental research task of Audio-Visual Event Localization (AVEL)~\cite{tian2018audio .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: For example, these methods aim to improve audio-visual fusion~\cite{tian2018audio,wu2019dual,xu2020cross,lin2019dual,duan2021audio,zhou2024dense,he2024cace,zhou2021positive , better distinguish the {background ~\cite{xia2022cross,zhou2022cpsp , and localize more precise temporal boundaries~\cite{yu2021mm,wu2022span,mahmud2023ave .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-287: As shown in the upper part of Table~\ref{tab:main_results , our training-free baseline outperforms both methods.}
elements.sent-2411-288: While Video-LLaMA2 can describe events within an {entire audio sequence, it faces challenges in achieving segment-level perception of audio-visual alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-289: Additionally, the comparison with {{CLIP\&CLAP highlights the advantages of using a joint feature space for multimodal feature embedding, which better captures semantic alignment among multiple modalities for the OV-AVEL task.}}
elements.sent-2411-291: We replace the temporal layers in our fine-tuning baseline with core audio-visual fusion modules from prior closed-set AVEL methods (e.g., CMRA~\cite{xu2020cross , AVE~\cite{tian2018audio , PSP~\cite{zhou2021positive , MM-Pyr~\cite{yu2021mm ) to enable them perform both seen and unseen event localization for comparison.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: As shown in the lower part of Table~\ref{tab:main_results , we find that: 1) While complex audio-visual interactions from prior methods may improve seen-class performance, they significantly degrade unseen-class performance, causing their overall results on the total test set to lag far behind ours.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Our fine-tuning baseline significantly outperforms the training-free version, showing an 11.2\% improvement in the average metric (`Avg.') on the total test set.

elements.sent-2411-299: In our fine-tuning baseline, we generate the audio-visual event probability $\bm{S _{ave ^{'}$ by computing the square root of the product of predicted audio event probability $\bm{S _{ae ^{'}$ and visual event probability $\bm{S _{ve ^{'}$ (Eq.~\ref{eq:fine_tune ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-300: Additionally, we find that both 100\% and 25\% of training data better improve unseen test data recognition, while using 50\% of training data is the most effective for seen test data recognition.
elements.sent-2411-301: These results reveal a {non-linear link between training data size and the model's performance, showing more data is not vital for seen/unseen class localization.}
elements.sent-2411-302: However, the trade-off of using less training data is the need for more training or fine-tuning epochs.
elements.sent-2411-303: These findings suggest that determining a more balanced training strategy to optimize both seen and unseen data recognition would be a valuable direction for future work.
